topic: Firmament Scaling Function from V12 geometry — verdict-narrative alignment after ISS-1023/1024 rewrite
change_summary: Queue item 138 (pushed 2026-04-15T03:44Z by decider after ISS-1023/1024 framing rewrite). Decider rewrote finding (133→297 chars), detail_verdict_text (93→609 chars), tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict, and code_analysis.post_hoc_evidence to lead with the Self-Contradicted version-instability narrative (HAD 1.47 vs 1.89; BOU 5,715 km → 9,983 km). detail_evidence (2,464 chars) was NOT touched and still opens with the c2/c3 Misleading-era consistency-not-truth framing — the exact risk the queue item asked curmudgeon to cross-check.
our_argument_summary: After the ISS-1023/1024 rewrite, the top-of-stack fields readers see — finding, detail_verdict_text, tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict — now lead with the version-instability self-contradiction (HAD FSF = 1.47 under V51 vs 1.89 under V50.6, a 28% swing; BOU reference radius shifting 5,715 → 9,983 km between dome versions). detail_verdict_text also cross-references Section 1.8's coordinate-collapse argument as a second structural contradiction. The Self-Contradicted verdict is now substantively defensible because a formula genuinely derived from a fixed (V12) geometry should not swing 28% when the author revises other parameters. The remaining weakness is internal to detail_evidence: its lead paragraph still argues 'internal derivation tests consistency, not truth' — the Misleading-era frame. For a reader who expands the panel and scans top-down, the first 600 characters undercut the punchier self-contradiction framing in the surrounding fields. The version-instability and check_eclipse_fsf.py source references do appear in detail_evidence, but only in the LAST paragraph, after two paragraphs of consistency-not-truth framing and a near-duplicated 'confirmed before tested is circular' paragraph. No hole yet found in the new self-contradiction claim itself, but defenders will notice the narrative inversion inside detail_evidence.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Dome publishes FSF per station (EBR 1.029, HAD 1.471, LER 2.075, NGK 1.529, CLF 1.325, SNK 0.642, COI 1.008, SPT 1.001, ESK 1.722) as (H(r_station)/r_station) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU). ΔB_station = baseline × coverage × FSF, where baseline is either the BOU primary (~-10.9 nT) or W004 secondary (~-22.24 nT). monitor.py applies this at eclipse event time; it does not compute FSF live — the values are tabled. check_eclipse_fsf.py (dome-side) and our independent re-derivation show the published FSF values reproduce only when the V51 ellipse H(r) is combined with V51 station radii; under V50.6 exponential H(r) with V50.6 radii the same formula produces materially different numbers (HAD 1.89 vs published 1.47).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hybrid. Published FSF values are hardcoded in the station table; no API or live fetch computes them. The H(r) function itself is computed from V12-claimed parameters but the parameter set that reproduces the published table is V51-specific. This is why version-instability is the right angle — the formula is algebraic but the parameters are version-fit.
data_sources_used: INTERMAGNET (future — eclipse-day 2026-08-12 readings for E001–E009, not yet available)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Dual baselines (BOU vs W004) produce roughly 2× spread in predicted ΔB per station; combined with ±2.1 nT tolerance bands, the successful-prediction window is wide. This is a separate concern from version-instability but would mute any observational verdict after the eclipse.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None new. The check_eclipse_fsf.py source reference added to detail_evidence matches the dome's repo usage. EXP-165 is the authoritative analyst record for the V50.6 vs V51 computation; its BOU radii (5,715 vs 9,983 km) and HAD-under-V50.6 value (1.89) are the basis for the 28% claim and are cited correctly.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: raw-text/monitor.py Domain 10 (line ~515) applies ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF at eclipse time using hardcoded per-station FSF values from the published E001–E009 table. No live INTERMAGNET fetch exists yet (would require 2026-08-12 eclipse data). The tag was previously marked "none" in wins.json — closer reading shows a static value table and a fixed formula, which fits hardcoded more precisely than none.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard geomagnetic eclipse models (e.g., Takeda & Yamada 1989; Curto et al. 2006) predict suppression of the Sq current during totality producing ΔB anomalies of order a few nT at magnetometer stations. They do not predict spatial variation of the form FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU). The dome's station-specific amplification factors are a dome-geometry-specific claim, not a relabel of standard physics.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The E001–E009 predictions were registered in the dome's repo before August 2026 and the observations (eclipse day) have not occurred yet. The version-instability problem is NOT post-hoc tuning; it is structural — same formula, different parameter sets, different output. post_hoc remains false; the correct pathology is self-contradiction, not retrodiction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF formula = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) uses the dome's H(r) firmament height function and dome coordinate radii. It has no standard-physics analog. However, the 'derivation' is only stable when the derivation inputs (H(r) profile and station radii) are frozen at V51 values — which is not what the detail_claim ('derives from Version 12 dome geometry') asserts. derives_from_dome is true but the derivation is not version-invariant.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence LEAD still uses Misleading-era consistency-not-truth framing. First ~600 characters open with: 'The formula is an internal model derivation: it is algebraically constructed from assumed dome parameters... Deriving a mathematical relationship from assumed geometry and then testing it against measured data amounts to testing the model's internal consistency, not testing the model against nature.' This is the c2 argument that motivated the Misleading verdict — it argues circularity of internal derivations, not self-contradiction. The new self-contradiction evidence (version-instability, BOU radius shift, Section 1.8 coordinate collapse) appears only in the LAST paragraph of detail_evidence. A reader who expands the panel top-down sees the old frame first and has to re-interpret it once they reach the bottom. The queue item's reason field explicitly asked curmudgeon to cross-check this: 'does detail_evidence still lead with consistency-not-truth? If so, rewrite lead to match new framing.' Answer: yes, it still does.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the first two paragraphs of detail_evidence to lead with the version-instability argument. Suggested opening: 'The FSF formula\'s output is version-unstable. Under V51 parameters (ellipse H(r), V51 station radii) the published values (EBR 1.029, HAD 1.471, LER 2.075, etc.) reproduce to within ~3%. Under V50.6 parameters (exponential H(r), V50.6 radii) the SAME formula produces HAD 1.89 — a 28% discrepancy at a single station. The BOU reference station that anchors the ratio moved from r = 5,715 km (V50.6) to r = 9,983 km (V51). A formula genuinely derived from V12 geometry should not swing 28% when the author revises other parameters. WIN-044\'s detail_claim asserts derivation from Version 12 geometry; the reproduction profile says the derivation is tied to V51-specific parameters.' Then keep the internal-consistency argument as a secondary point, not the lead.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence contains near-duplicate paragraphs. 'Claiming a formula as a confirmed WIN before the observations that would test it is circular: the formula is confirmed because it was derived, not because it was tested' appears twice in consecutive paragraphs, once as 'Moreover, the eclipse predictions...' and immediately again as 'Claiming the FSF formula as a confirmed WIN before a single per-station eclipse reading validates it is circular...'. The second restatement adds nothing factual. After the lead rewrite (Hole 1) this redundancy becomes doubly visible — two paragraphs restating a now-secondary argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Collapse the two 'circular until August 2026' paragraphs into a single sentence once the version-instability lead is in place. The second sentence's 'until then, this WIN confirms nothing beyond internal algebraic consistency' is worth keeping; the rest is duplicative.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence does not cross-reference Section 1.8 (coordinate collapse), even though detail_verdict_text now explicitly invokes it as the 'second structural contradiction.' The evidence body supporting the verdict should name the same argument the verdict text rests on. Currently the two fields use different supporting arguments: evidence leans on version-instability alone; verdict_text adds Section 1.8. Section 1.8 itself lists WIN-044 among contaminated WINs, so the linkage is already mutual on the Section-1.8 side — only WIN-044's own detail_evidence fails to return the reference.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a short paragraph (2–3 sentences) at the end of detail_evidence naming Section 1.8 as the second contradiction and linking to it (use the part1-1.8 anchor or whatever the current anchor scheme is). Keep it brief — the full argument lives in Section 1.8, WIN-044 just needs to cite it.

holes_found.description: code_analysis.monitoring is tagged 'none' in wins.json but the substantive content in raw-text/monitor.py Domain 10 (line ~515) applies a hardcoded per-station FSF table in the ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF formula at eclipse time. Per the curmudgeon tag rubric, 'monitor.py contains a static expected value AND a static observed value' = hardcoded; 'none' is reserved for WINs that monitor.py does not touch at all. Domain 10 DOES touch this WIN — it just uses static values on both sides. The correct tag is hardcoded, not none. (The observations won't exist until 2026-08-12, so the 'observed' slot is currently empty, which is why past reviewers may have chosen none — but the monitor harness does claim this WIN's slot.)
holes_found.recommendation: Flag for decider/analyst: consider re-tagging code_analysis.monitoring from 'none' to 'hardcoded' once eclipse day passes and the observed slot is populated. Low urgency (observations not yet collected), but worth an ISS ticket so the tag updates automatically post-eclipse. Do not edit the tag now — let the analyst decide with full context.

holes_found.description: Dual-baseline tolerance (BOU -10.9 nT vs W004 -22.24 nT primary baselines, producing roughly 2× spread in per-station ΔB windows plus ±~2.1 nT tolerance) is still not quantified inside WIN-044's own text. Raised in c2 and c3. This dilutes any future eclipse-day observational test: with two baselines and station-specific tolerances, nearly any observed ΔB near the expected range will 'confirm' a prediction. Not a verdict-changer now — the Self-Contradicted verdict is about pre-observation self-contradiction, not post-observation tolerance gaming — but if the verdict ever evolves after 2026-08-12 data arrives, this structural weakness should be named.
holes_found.recommendation: Defer until eclipse data arrives. Optionally add a one-sentence parenthetical in detail_evidence noting that the baseline ambiguity will also need to be addressed at verdict-update time.

holes_found.description: detail_extra remains null. c2 and c3 both suggested a short mathematical exposition block (formula, station values 0.642–2.075, baseline ΔB structure). The version-instability rewrite did not add one. Low priority; most readers do not expand detail_extra anyway.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional. If a short exposition is added, it is the natural home for the E001–E009 value table plus the V50.6 vs V51 parameter comparison, which would shorten detail_evidence.

stronger_arguments.description: The version-instability argument can be strengthened by naming the direction of the parameter change. Between V50.6 and V51 the dome author changed H(r) from an exponential profile (H = 8,537 × exp(-r/8,619)) to an ellipse profile, AND changed the station radii. If the FSF were truly derived from V12 geometry, only ONE of these parameter sets could match the V12 definition; the other is a re-parameterization. Either V50.6 or V51 must be mis-labeled as V12-derived. This sharpens 'version-unstable' from 'output changes' to 'the V12-derivation label is logically inconsistent with having two parameter sets that produce different outputs.'

Stronger single-sentence punch for the TLDR: 'The same formula gives HAD = 1.47 in V51 and HAD = 1.89 in V50.6; a derivation that depends on which dome version you use cannot also claim to come from fixed V12 geometry.' Current tldr_verdict is close to this but does not name V12 directly, which is the self-contradictory label.

kernel_of_truth.description: The FSF formula (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) IS a legitimate internal algebraic construct — given H(r) and station radii, it computes a single number per station in a well-defined way. The dome author's insight that eclipse magnetic anomalies might spatially vary is also empirically reasonable: spatial variation of the ΔB during totality is observed in real eclipse datasets. What the dome gets right is the intuition that a station-specific amplification factor might exist.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being a well-defined algebraic object says nothing about whether the object is uniquely determined by V12 geometry. The version-instability test shows it is not uniquely determined — two parameter sets produce materially different values for the same station. And the intuition about station-specific ΔB variation is shared with standard geomagnetic models (Sq current suppression varies with local magnetic latitude and eclipse geometry), so the form of the dome's prediction competes with a standard-physics prediction of similar structure rather than filling a gap standard physics leaves open.

deeper_analysis: The ISS-1023/1024 rewrite successfully repointed the top-of-stack fields (finding, detail_verdict_text, both TLDRs, post_hoc_evidence) to the version-instability narrative. The Self-Contradicted verdict is now substantively earned: if a formula's output changes by 28% at the same station when other parameters change between dome versions, the 'derived from V12 geometry' label is either wrong or the derivation is hidden in parameters that move between versions. Either reading is a self-contradiction with the detail_claim. The remaining hole is confined to detail_evidence — it is the longest reader-facing field and retains the c2-era argument structure. Because summary fields now lead with self-contradiction but the body still leads with consistency-not-truth, a careful reader experiences a narrative inversion inside a single panel. A dome defender could use this to argue that our review is unsettled about WHY this WIN fails — which is the kind of inconsistency we criticize the dome for. The fix is mechanical (rewrite two paragraphs, delete a duplicated paragraph) and does not require a verdict change. On the other side: a defender could argue that the version-instability evidence is OUR re-computation (via check_fsf.py) rather than a dome-published contradiction, and that the dome author would simply declare 'the FSF formula is a V51 object, not a V12 object.' That defense has force but is self-defeating: it either concedes the detail_claim ('derives from Version 12 dome geometry') is wrong, or it admits the formula is parameter-fit at each version. Either admission is the self-contradiction we claim; the defender cannot have both a V12 derivation and version-stable output. We should make sure the rewritten detail_evidence lead forces the dome into this dilemma explicitly.

citations_verified: E001–E009 FSF values in raw-text/03-predictions.txt (EBR 1.029, HAD 1.471, LER 2.075, NGK 1.529, CLF 1.325, SNK 0.642, COI 1.008, SPT 1.001, ESK 1.722) — match detail_evidence

FSF formula ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF — raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 21–23
